question:
Let $P(x) = c_n x^n + c_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \cdots + c_0$ be a polynomial with
integer coefficients. Suppose that $r$ is a rational number such that
$P(r) = 0$.  Show that the $n$ numbers
\begin{gather*}
c_n r, \, c_n r^2 + c_{n-1} r, \, c_n r^3 + c_{n-1} r^2 + c_{n-2} r, \\
\dots, \, c_n r^n + c_{n-1} r^{n-1} + \cdots + c_1 r
\end{gather*}
are integers.
solution:
Let $k$ be an integer, $0\leq k\leq n-1$. Since $P(r)/r^k = 0$, we
have
\begin{multline*}
c_n r^{n-k} + c_{n-1} r^{n-k+1} + \dots + c_{k+1} r \\
= - (c_k + c_{k-1} r^{-1} + \dots + c_0 r^{-k}).
\end{multline*}
Write $r = p/q$ where $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime. Then the
left hand side of the above equation can be written as a fraction
with denominator $q^{n-k}$, while the right hand side is a fraction
with denominator $p^k$. Since $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime, both
sides of the equation must be an integer, and the result follows.

\textbf{Remark:}
If we write $r = a/b$ in lowest terms, then $P(x)$ factors as $(bx-a)Q(x)$,
where the polynomial $Q$ has integer coefficients because you can
either do the long division from the left and get denominators divisible only
by primes dividing $b$, or do it from the right and get denominators
divisible only by primes dividing $a$. The numbers given in the problem
are none other than $a$ times the coefficients of $Q$.
More generally, if $P(x)$ is divisible,
as a polynomial over the rationals, by a polynomial $R(x)$ with integer
coefficients, then $P/R$ also has integer coefficients; this is known
as ``Gauss's lemma'' and holds in any unique factorization domain.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $polynomm(algebraic) = leadcoef\, algebraic^{degree} + c_{degree-1} \, algebraic^{degree-1} + \cdots + zerocoef$ be a polynomial with
integer coefficients. Suppose that $rational$ is a rational number such that
$polynomm(rational) = 0$.  Show that the $degree$ numbers
\begin{gather*}
leadcoef\, rational, \, leadcoef\, rational^2 + c_{degree-1} \, rational, \, leadcoef\, rational^3 + c_{degree-1} \, rational^2 + c_{degree-2} \, rational, \\
\dots, \, leadcoef\, rational^{degree} + c_{degree-1} \, rational^{degree-1} + \cdots + firstcoef\, rational
\end{gather*}
are integers.
descriptive_long.solution:
Let $indexer$ be an integer, $0\leq indexer\leq degree-1$. Since $polynomm(rational)/rational^{indexer} = 0$, we
have
\begin{multline*}
leadcoef\, rational^{degree-indexer} + c_{degree-1} \, rational^{degree-indexer+1} + \dots + c_{indexer+1} \, rational \\
= - (kcoeff + c_{indexer-1} \, rational^{-1} + \dots + zerocoef\, rational^{-indexer}).
\end{multline*}
Write $rational = numeratr/denominr$ where $numeratr$ and $denominr$ are relatively prime. Then the
left hand side of the above equation can be written as a fraction
with denominator $denominr^{degree-indexer}$, while the right hand side is a fraction
with denominator $numeratr^{indexer}$. Since $numeratr$ and $denominr$ are relatively prime, both
sides of the equation must be an integer, and the result follows.

\textbf{Remark:}
If we write $rational = coeffic/baseterm$ in lowest terms, then $polynomm(algebraic)$ factors as $(baseterm algebraic-coeffic)auxpoly(algebraic)$,
where the polynomial $auxpoly$ has integer coefficients because you can
either do the long division from the left and get denominators divisible only
by primes dividing $baseterm$, or do it from the right and get denominators
divisible only by primes dividing $coeffic$. The numbers given in the problem
are none other than $coeffic$ times the coefficients of $auxpoly$.
More generally, if $polynomm(algebraic)$ is divisible,
as a polynomial over the rationals, by a polynomial $divisor(algebraic)$ with integer
coefficients, then $polynomm/divisor$ also has integer coefficients; this is known
as ``Gauss's lemma'' and holds in any unique factorization domain.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $umbrella(chocolate) = porcelain chocolate^{labyrinth} + kaleidoscope chocolate^{labyrinth-1} + \cdots + strawberry$ be a polynomial with
integer coefficients. Suppose that $sapphire$ is a rational number such that
$umbrella(sapphire) = 0$.  Show that the $labyrinth$ numbers
\begin{gather*}
porcelain sapphire, \, porcelain sapphire^2 + kaleidoscope sapphire, \, porcelain sapphire^3 + kaleidoscope sapphire^2 + thunderbolt sapphire, \\
\dots, \, porcelain sapphire^{labyrinth} + kaleidoscope sapphire^{labyrinth-1} + \cdots + butterfly sapphire
\end{gather*}
are integers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Let $telescope$ be an integer, $0\leq telescope\leq labyrinth-1$. Since $umbrella(sapphire)/sapphire^{telescope} = 0$, we
have
\begin{multline*}
porcelain sapphire^{labyrinth-telescope} + kaleidoscope sapphire^{labyrinth-telescope+1} + \dots + rainforest sapphire \\
= - (silhouette + skyscraper sapphire^{-1} + \dots + strawberry sapphire^{-telescope}).
\end{multline*}
Write $sapphire = gardenia/marigold$ where $gardenia$ and $marigold$ are relatively prime. Then the
left hand side of the above equation can be written as a fraction
with denominator $marigold^{labyrinth-telescope}$, while the right hand side is a fraction
with denominator $gardenia^{telescope}$. Since $gardenia$ and $marigold$ are relatively prime, both
sides of the equation must be an integer, and the result follows.

\textbf{Remark:}
If we write $sapphire = elephant/harmonic$ in lowest terms, then $umbrella(chocolate)$ factors as $(harmonic chocolate-elephant)necklace(chocolate)$,
where the polynomial $necklace$ has integer coefficients because you can
either do the long division from the left and get denominators divisible only
by primes dividing $harmonic$, or do it from the right and get denominators
divisible only by primes dividing $elephant$. The numbers given in the problem
are none other than $elephant$ times the coefficients of $necklace$.
More generally, if $umbrella(chocolate)$ is divisible,
as a polynomial over the rationals, by a polynomial $planetarium(chocolate)$ with integer
coefficients, then $umbrella/planetarium$ also has integer coefficients; this is known
as ``Gauss's lemma'' and holds in any unique factorization domain.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $quotientpoly(constantvar) = trailingcoeff constantvar^{baselength} + reversecoeff constantvar^{baselength-1} + \cdots + leadingcoeff$ be a polynomial with
integer coefficients. Suppose that $irrationalnum$ is a rational number such that
$quotientpoly(irrationalnum) = 0$.  Show that the $baselength$ numbers
\begin{gather*}
trailingcoeff irrationalnum, \, trailingcoeff irrationalnum^2 + reversecoeff irrationalnum, \, trailingcoeff irrationalnum^3 + reversecoeff irrationalnum^2 + backwardcoeff irrationalnum, \\
\dots, \, trailingcoeff irrationalnum^{baselength} + reversecoeff irrationalnum^{baselength-1} + \cdots + frontcoeff irrationalnum
\end{gather*}
are integers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Let $unboundedindex$ be an integer, $0\leq unboundedindex\leq baselength-1$. Since $quotientpoly(irrationalnum)/irrationalnum^{unboundedindex} = 0$, we
have
\begin{multline*}
trailingcoeff irrationalnum^{baselength-unboundedindex} + reversecoeff irrationalnum^{baselength-unboundedindex+1} + \dots + fixedcoeff irrationalnum \\
= - (variablecoeff + stablecoeff irrationalnum^{-1} + \dots + leadingcoeff irrationalnum^{-unboundedindex}).
\end{multline*}
Write $irrationalnum = compositepart/numeratorval$ where $compositepart$ and $numeratorval$ are relatively prime. Then the
left hand side of the above equation can be written as a fraction
with denominator $numeratorval^{baselength-unboundedindex}$, while the right hand side is a fraction
with denominator $compositepart^{unboundedindex}$. Since $compositepart$ and $numeratorval$ are relatively prime, both
sides of the equation must be an integer, and the result follows.

\textbf{Remark:}
If we write $irrationalnum = divisornum/numeratorcoeff$ in lowest terms, then $quotientpoly(x)$ factors as $(numeratorcoeff x-divisornum)multiplepoly(x)$,
where the polynomial $multiplepoly$ has integer coefficients because you can
either do the long division from the left and get denominators divisible only
by primes dividing $numeratorcoeff$, or do it from the right and get denominators
divisible only by primes dividing $divisornum$. The numbers given in the problem
are none other than $divisornum$ times the coefficients of $multiplepoly$.
More generally, if $quotientpoly(x)$ is divisible,
as a polynomial over the rationals, by a polynomial $dividendpoly(x)$ with integer
coefficients, then $quotientpoly/dividendpoly$ also has integer coefficients; this is known
as ``Gauss's lemma'' and holds in any unique factorization domain.

garbled_string.question:
Let $kzjfeoaw(gfhqrnle) = prhslmca gfhqrnle^{ecxjodpv} + mtcvarzo gfhqrnle^{ecxjodpv-1} + \cdots + akwmnofs$ be a polynomial with
integer coefficients. Suppose that $dfjnsqmn$ is a rational number such that
$kzjfeoaw(dfjnsqmn) = 0$.  Show that the $ecxjodpv$ numbers
\begin{gather*}
prhslmca dfjnsqmn, \, prhslmca dfjnsqmn^2 + mtcvarzo dfjnsqmn, \, prhslmca dfjnsqmn^3 + mtcvarzo dfjnsqmn^2 + fydgeqhi dfjnsqmn, \\
\dots, \, prhslmca dfjnsqmn^{ecxjodpv} + mtcvarzo dfjnsqmn^{ecxjodpv-1} + \cdots + znpevktu dfjnsqmn
\end{gather*}
are integers.
garbled_string.solution:
Let $zbcptewu$ be an integer, $0\leq zbcptewu\leq ecxjodpv-1$. Since $kzjfeoaw(dfjnsqmn)/dfjnsqmn^{zbcptewu} = 0$, we
have
\begin{multline*}
prhslmca dfjnsqmn^{ecxjodpv-zbcptewu} + mtcvarzo dfjnsqmn^{ecxjodpv-zbcptewu+1} + \dots + ojsylrax dfjnsqmn \\
= - (qmebvufi + shzinwdo dfjnsqmn^{-1} + \dots + akwmnofs dfjnsqmn^{-zbcptewu}).
\end{multline*}
Write $dfjnsqmn = jqlgadzr/xnrymbvo$ where $jqlgadzr$ and $xnrymbvo$ are relatively prime. Then the
left hand side of the above equation can be written as a fraction
with denominator $xnrymbvo^{ecxjodpv-zbcptewu}$, while the right hand side is a fraction
with denominator $jqlgadzr^{zbcptewu}$. Since $jqlgadzr$ and $xnrymbvo$ are relatively prime, both
sides of the equation must be an integer, and the result follows.

\textbf{Remark:}
If we write $dfjnsqmn = vhrscmea/wduplitk$ in lowest terms, then $kzjfeoaw(gfhqrnle)$ factors as $(wduplitk gfhqrnle-vhrscmea)uecrsbij(gfhqrnle)$,
where the polynomial $uecrsbij$ has integer coefficients because you can
either do the long division from the left and get denominators divisible only
by primes dividing $wduplitk$, or do it from the right and get denominators
divisible only by primes dividing $vhrscmea$. The numbers given in the problem
are none other than $vhrscmea$ times the coefficients of $uecrsbij$.
More generally, if $kzjfeoaw(gfhqrnle)$ is divisible,
as a polynomial over the rationals, by a polynomial $difplazm(gfhqrnle)$ with integer
coefficients, then $kzjfeoaw/difplazm$ also has integer coefficients; this is known
as ``Gauss's lemma'' and holds in any unique factorization domain.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 1$ be an integer and
\[
P(x)=a_0+a_1x+\dots +a_{d}x^{d}\qquad(a_0,a_1,\dots ,a_d\in\mathbb Z)
\]
be a polynomial with integer coefficients.  Suppose the rational number $r$ satisfies $P(r)=0$.  Prove that each of the following $d$ numbers
\[
\begin{gathered}
 a_{d},\\
 a_{d}r+a_{d-1},\\
 a_{d}r^{2}+a_{d-1}r+a_{d-2},\\
 \vdots\\
 a_{d}r^{d-1}+a_{d-1}r^{d-2}+\cdots +a_{1}
\end{gathered}
\]
is an integer.
kernel_variant.solution:
First handle the trivial case r=0.  If P(0)=0 then a_0=0, and the d numbers
  a_d,
  a_d\cdot 0 + a_{d-1} = a_{d-1},
  a_d\cdot 0^2 + a_{d-1}\cdot 0 + a_{d-2} = a_{d-2}, \ldots , a_1
are all integers.  Hence the conclusion holds when r=0.

Now assume r\neq 0.  Write
  r = b/a  in lowest terms (so a and b are nonzero integers, gcd(a,b)=1).  Since P(r)=0 we have
  a_0 + a_1(b/a) + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + a_d(b/a)^d = 0.

Fix t\in {1,2,\ldots ,d} and divide this equation by r^{d+1-t}:

  a_d r^{t-1} + a_{d-1} r^{t-2} + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + a_{d-t+1}
    = -(a_{d-t} r^{-1} + a_{d-t-1} r^{-2} + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + a_0 r^{-(d+1-t)}).

On the left, each term a_{d-j+1}r^{t-j} = a_{d-j+1}(b/a)^{t-j} has denominator dividing a^{t-j} \leq  a^{t-1}.  Hence the entire left side is a rational number whose denominator divides a^{t-1}.
On the right, each term a_{d-j}r^{-(j+1)} = a_{d-j}(b/a)^{-(j+1)} = a_{d-j}\cdot a^{j+1}/b^{j+1} has denominator dividing b^{j+1} \leq  b^{d+1-t}.  Hence the right side is a rational number whose denominator divides b^{d+1-t}.

Since gcd(a^{t-1},b^{d+1-t})=1, a rational number whose denominator divides a^{t-1} and at the same time divides b^{d+1-t} must in fact be an integer.  Therefore

  a_d r^{t-1} + a_{d-1} r^{t-2} + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + a_{d-t+1} \in  \mathbb{Z}.

As t runs from 1 to d, these are exactly the d numbers
  a_d,
  a_d r + a_{d-1},
  a_d r^2 + a_{d-1} r + a_{d-2},
  \ldots ,
  a_d r^{d-1} + a_{d-1} r^{d-2} + \cdot \cdot \cdot  + a_1,

each of which is therefore an integer, as desired. \blacksquare

core_steps: Start from P(r)=0 and divide by r^k to isolate two complementary coefficient–power blocks.
Express r in lowest terms r = p/q with (p,q)=1.
Observe denominators: left side has q^{n-k}, right side has p^{k}.
Coprimality forces each side—and hence every isolated partial sum—to be an integer.
Choose k=1,2,…,n to obtain the n stated expressions.

slot1.description: Symbol that denotes the degree of the polynomial (currently ‘n’). Any positive integer symbol could be used without affecting the argument.

slot2.description: Index range for k that sweeps the complementary blocks (currently 0 ≤ k ≤ n−1). Any equivalent range that covers all non-trivial splits, e.g. 1 ≤ k ≤ n, would work.

slot3.description: Notation for the reduced form of the root (currently r = p/q with p,q coprime). Different letters or the order p/q vs. q/p could be used so long as coprimality is kept.

slot4.description: Choice of dividing by r^k (thus creating exponents n−k on one side and −k on the other). Any systematic split—e.g. dividing by r^{n−k}—would yield equivalent complementary sums.

slot5.description: Presentation order of the integer expressions (currently first power 1 up to n). Any permutation or reverse order would not impact the logical chain.
slot5.original: c_n r, c_n r^2 + c_{n-1} r, … , c_n r^n + … + c_1 r
